task: I’m planning a special-occasion future trip to New York City and I don’t want just three fancy hotel names — I want a real luxury-hotel decision I could actually book from. Start with Google Hotels or Google Travel and build me a comparison set of exactly 12 NYC luxury hotels that are clearly positioned at the top end of the market, ideally true 5-star properties when that’s shown. Use the same future 3-night stay window for all 12 so the prices are comparable, and record the nightly rate or average nightly rate shown there, plus any resort, destination, or mandatory fees if they’re publicly shown. Make sure the 12 hotels cover at least 4 distinct NYC luxury-stay areas such as Central Park South, Midtown/Fifth Avenue, Downtown/Tribeca/SoHo, and one other area if relevant. Then open the official property pages for the 8 strongest contenders and verify the core details that matter for an actual decision: room style and size cues if shown, standout amenities like spa/pool, dining on-site, view/location advantages, and cancellation flexibility if publicly listed. For those 8 contenders, also use maps and photos to sanity-check what the surrounding area feels like and how convenient each one is for a luxury leisure trip. After that, narrow the list to a final 6-hotel shortlist and give me a structured recommendation: best overall luxury stay, best classic NYC splurge, best quieter or more private option, and best value-for-luxury among the finalists. Please keep the most useful tabs open at the end: the Google hotel results page for the search, the official pages for the final 6 hotels, and at least 2 map or photo views that help show the neighborhood tradeoffs.
grader_reasoning:
R1: success - The agent successfully established a 3-night future stay window (August 7–10, 2026) in Google Hotels. It then applied a 5-star filter to identify luxury properties. In its final response (Step 56), the agent provided a structured table containing exactly 12 luxury hotels, each with a corresponding nightly rate derived from the Google Hotels search workflow. The requirement for a comparison set of 12 hotels with rates for a consistent future window is satisfied.

R2: success - The agent successfully built a comparison set of 12 luxury hotels in NYC. In step 56, the agent provided a table listing the 12 hotels with their respective areas:
1. Aman New York (Midtown / Fifth Ave)
2. The Plaza (Central Park South / Fifth Ave)
3. Waldorf Astoria New York (Midtown East / Park Ave)
4. Crosby Street Hotel (SoHo)
5. The Mercer (SoHo)
6. The Ritz-Carlton New York, Central Park (Central Park South)
7. The Surrey, A Corinthia Hotel (Upper East Side)
8. Four Seasons Hotel New York Downtown (Tribeca / Downtown)
9. The Carlyle, A Rosewood Hotel (Upper East Side)
10. Mandarin Oriental, New York (Columbus Circle / Central Park)
11. The Langham, New York, Fifth Avenue (Midtown / Fifth Ave)
12. The Fifth Avenue Hotel (NoMad / Fifth Ave)

These cover the required areas: Central Park South (The Plaza, Ritz-Carlton, Mandarin Oriental), Midtown/Fifth Avenue (Aman, Langham, Fifth Avenue Hotel, Waldorf Astoria), Downtown/Tribeca/SoHo (Crosby Street, The Mercer, Four Seasons Downtown), and an additional area (Upper East Side - The Surrey, The Carlyle). The requirement for 4 distinct areas is satisfied.

R3: success - The agent successfully built a comparison table of 12 luxury hotels in NYC. In the final response (Step 56), the agent explicitly included a column for "Fee note" and populated it for all 12 hotels, noting where fees were found (e.g., The Plaza) or stating that no mandatory fees were found in the public pages checked for the others. This satisfies the requirement to record fee information when publicly shown and to clearly note missing cases.

R4: success - The agent successfully identified 8 luxury hotel contenders and performed web searches to gather decision-critical details for each, including room style, amenities (spa/pool/dining), and cancellation policies. The agent then opened the official pages for these properties (as evidenced by the `openTab` calls in step 51 and the final tab list in step 54). The information gathered was synthesized into the final recommendation provided in the response.

R5: success - The agent successfully opened Google Maps views for the relevant areas (Central Park South and Downtown/Tribeca) to evaluate the neighborhood tradeoffs for the luxury hotels. The action history shows the agent specifically navigating to these map URLs (Step 51, 52, 53) and the final response incorporates these neighborhood considerations into the structured recommendation. This satisfies the requirement to use maps/photos to sanity-check the surrounding area and leisure-trip convenience for the contenders.

R6: success - The agent successfully produced a final 6-hotel shortlist in its final response (Step 56). The recommendation includes the four required categories: "Best overall luxury stay" (Aman New York), "Best classic NYC splurge" (The Plaza), "Best quieter or more private option" (The Carlyle), and "Best value-for-luxury" (The Fifth Avenue Hotel). The list is narrowed to exactly 6 finalists as requested.

R7: success - The agent successfully navigated to the Google Hotels search results page with the correct dates and filters applied. It then opened the official websites for the final 6 hotels (Aman, The Plaza, Ritz-Carlton, Mandarin Oriental, The Carlyle, and Four Seasons Downtown) and 2 Google Maps views for neighborhood tradeoffs. The final browser state contains exactly the tabs required by the rubric.
